title: Present Perfect (Perfekt)
description: The Perfekt is the most common past tense in spoken German. It is formed with an auxiliary verb (haben or sein) in position 2 and a past participle (Partizip II) at the end of the clause.
whenToUse: Talking about completed past actions in spoken German: Ich habe gestern Pizza gegessen.
Describing personal experiences: Ich bin schon einmal nach Japan geflogen.
Reporting recent events: Es hat heute geregnet.
In informal writing such as emails or text messages: Hast du meine Nachricht bekommen?

rules.title: Choosing the Auxiliary: haben vs. sein
rules.explanation: Most verbs use haben. Sein is used with verbs of movement from A to B (gehen, fahren, fliegen), verbs indicating a change of state (einschlafen, aufwachen, sterben), and the verbs sein, werden, bleiben.

examples.explanation: Lesen does not indicate movement or change of state, so it takes haben.
Fahren describes movement from A to B, so it takes sein.
Einschlafen indicates a change of state (awake → asleep), so it takes sein.

rules.title: Past Participle of Regular (Weak) Verbs
rules.explanation: Regular verbs form the Partizip II with the prefix ge- and the ending -t added to the verb stem: ge- + stem + -t. Example: machen → gemacht, spielen → gespielt.

examples.explanation: Spielen is regular: ge- + spiel + -t = gespielt.
Warten is regular but has a stem ending in -t, so the participle is ge- + wart + -et = gewartet.
Öffnen is regular: ge- + öffn + -et = geöffnet (extra -e- because stem ends in -n after a consonant).

rules.title: Past Participle of Irregular (Strong) Verbs
rules.explanation: Strong verbs form the Partizip II with ge- and the ending -en, often with a vowel change in the stem: ge- + changed stem + -en. These must be memorized. Example: trinken → getrunken, schreiben → geschrieben.

examples.explanation: Schreiben is strong: ge- + schrieb + -en = geschrieben (ei → ie vowel change).
Trinken is strong: ge- + trunk + -en = getrunken (i → u vowel change).
Sehen is strong: ge- + seh + -en = gesehen (no vowel change in this case).

rules.title: Inseparable Prefix Verbs: No ge- in Partizip II
rules.explanation: Verbs with inseparable prefixes (be-, emp-, ent-, er-, ge-, miss-, ver-, zer-) do NOT add ge- to the past participle. The prefix stays attached.

examples.explanation: Besuchen has the inseparable prefix be-, so the participle is besucht (not gebesucht).

examples.correct: Er hat die Frage nicht verstanden.
examples.incorrect: Er hat die Frage nicht geverstanden.
examples.explanation: Verstehen has the inseparable prefix ver-, so the participle is verstanden.
Unterschreiben has an inseparable prefix, so no ge-: unterschrieben.

rules.title: Word Order in Perfekt
rules.explanation: The auxiliary verb (haben/sein) takes position 2 in the main clause, and the past participle goes to the very end of the clause.

examples.correct: Ich habe gestern einen Film gesehen.
examples.incorrect: Ich habe gesehen gestern einen Film.
examples.explanation: The past participle (gesehen) must go to the end, not directly after the auxiliary.
Even with a time expression in position 1, the auxiliary stays in position 2 and the participle at the end.

commonMistakes.explanation: Gehen indicates movement from A to B, so it requires sein, not haben.

commonMistakes.correct: Sie hat das Buch besucht. / Er hat die Stadt besucht.
commonMistakes.explanation: Inseparable prefix verbs (be-, ver-, er-, ent-, etc.) never get ge- in the past participle.
The past participle must be at the very end of the main clause, after all other elements.
Verbs ending in -ieren never take ge-: studiert, telefoniert, repariert.
Bleiben takes sein even though there is no movement, because it belongs to the sein/werden/bleiben group.
For separable verbs, ge- goes between the prefix and the stem: auf-ge-räumt, not ge-auf-räumt.

tips: In spoken German, the Perfekt is almost always used instead of the Präteritum, except for sein (war), haben (hatte), and modal verbs (konnte, musste, etc.).
Memorize the most common strong verb participles in groups based on vowel patterns: trinken-getrunken, finden-gefunden, singen-gesungen (i → u pattern).
If you are unsure whether a verb takes haben or sein, haben is the safer guess — it covers about 80% of verbs.
Remember the -ieren rule: verbs ending in -ieren never take ge- (studiert, telefoniert, funktioniert).
Practice the word order: auxiliary in position 2, everything else in between, participle at the very end.
